---
title: "Tuning tutorial"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Tuning tutorial}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

```{r}
library(ineqTrees)
```

<!-- WARNING - This vignette is generated by {fusen} from dev/tuning_tutorial.Rmd: do not edit by hand -->



<!--
 Source of truth for the tuning tutorial pkgdown article.
 This file is intentionally a short narrative vignette. It does not define
 package functions. Run the inflate chunk at the bottom after editing.
-->

# Introduction

This short tutorial shows the current cross-validation API for greedy
concentration-index trees and concentration-index forests. The core workflow is
the same for both model classes:

1. Build a small control grid.
2. Run cross-validation with `tune_ci_tree()` or `tune_ci_forest()`.
3. Select settings with `ci_select_best()`.
4. Collect metrics with `ci_collect_metrics()`.
5. Build a report-ready table with `ci_fit_summary_table()`.

The examples use the simulated Kenya child-survival data included with
`ineqTrees`.
```{r setup}
library(ineqTrees)
library(data.table)
library(knitr)

data(kenya, package = "ineqTrees")
setDT(kenya)

tuning_vars <- c(
  "wealth",
  "deadu5_num",
  "rural",
  "ed",
  "reg",
  "unskilled",
  "sample_weight"
)

tuning_data <- kenya[
  complete.cases(kenya[, ..tuning_vars]),
  ..tuning_vars
]

set.seed(20260521)
tuning_data <- tuning_data[
  sample.int(.N, min(700L, .N))
]

ci_formula <- cbind(wealth, deadu5_num) ~ rural + ed + reg + unskilled
criterion_types <- c("CI", "CIg")
tuning_metrics <- c(
  "validation_gain",
  "relative_validation_gain",
  "brier"
)
```


`validation_gain` is the reduction in validation-fold concentration-index
impurity after observations are assigned to terminal nodes. The relative metric
scales that gain by the absolute validation root impurity:

```r
relative_validation_gain = validation_gain / abs(root_impurity)
```

The raw gain is useful for selecting within a criterion. The relative gain and
the percentage gain columns are useful for interpreting how much validation
root impurity the model recovers.

## Cross-Validate Trees

Start with a grid of greedy tree controls. These are split-search controls, not
conditional-inference test controls.
```{r tree-grid}
tree_grid <- ci_tree_control_grid(
  minsplit = c(120L, 180L),
  minbucket = 60L,
  minprob = 0.05,
  maxdepth = 2:3
)

tree_grid
```


Run cross-validation with `tune_ci_tree()`. The first metric is the selection
metric used for `tree_tuning$best_fit` when `refit = TRUE`.
```{r tune-tree}
tree_tuning <- tune_ci_tree(
  formula = ci_formula,
  data = tuning_data,
  rank_name = "wealth",
  outcome_name = "deadu5_num",
  weights = tuning_data$sample_weight,
  type = criterion_types,
  control_grid = tree_grid,
  v = 3L,
  strata = "deadu5_num",
  seed = 20260521,
  metrics = tuning_metrics,
  control = control_ci_tune(save_pred = TRUE),
  refit = TRUE
)
```


Use `ci_collect_metrics(format = "wide")` when you want one row per setting
with metric columns.
```{r tree-wide-metrics}
tree_metrics_wide <- ci_collect_metrics(
  tree_tuning,
  metric = tuning_metrics,
  format = "wide"
)

knitr::kable(
  tree_metrics_wide[
    order(-mean_validation_gain),
    .(
      type,
      minsplit,
      minbucket,
      maxdepth,
      mean_validation_gain,
      std_err_validation_gain,
      mean_validation_relative_gain,
      mean_brier
    )
  ],
  digits = 4,
  caption = "Cross-validated tree metrics"
)
```


Use `ci_select_best()` to keep the best setting within each criterion type.
```{r tree-select-best}
tree_best <- ci_select_best(
  tree_tuning,
  metric = "validation_gain"
)

knitr::kable(
  tree_best[
    ,
    .(type, minsplit, minbucket, minprob, maxdepth)
  ],
  digits = 4,
  caption = "Selected tree controls"
)
```


For reports, `ci_fit_summary_table()` combines selected settings, validation
metrics, training diagnostics, and root-objective summaries.
```{r tree-fit-summary}
tree_fit_summary <- ci_fit_summary_table(
  tree_tuning,
  selected = tree_best,
  metrics = c(
    "train_gain",
    "validation_gain",
    "train_relative_gain",
    "relative_validation_gain"
  ),
  include_percent = TRUE
)

knitr::kable(
  tree_fit_summary[
    ,
    .(
      type,
      mean_root_objective,
      mean_train_gain,
      mean_percent_train_gain,
      mean_validation_gain,
      mean_percent_validation_gain,
      std_err_validation_gain
    )
  ],
  digits = 4,
  caption = "Selected tree fit summary"
)
```


Saved validation predictions are available when `save_pred = TRUE`.
```{r tree-predictions}
head(
  ci_collect_predictions(tree_tuning)[
    ,
    .(grid_id, fold_id, type, row_id, outcome, .pred)
  ]
)
```


The selected refit is stored on the tuning object.
```{r selected-tree-summary}
ci_tree_terminal_summary(tree_tuning$best_fit)[
  ,
  .(node, n, depth, ci, outcome_percent, rule)
]
```


## Cross-Validate Forests

Forest tuning uses the same metric and collector API. The forest grid also
includes `ntree` and `mtry`.
```{r forest-grid}
forest_grid <- ci_tree_control_grid(
  minsplit = 120L,
  minbucket = 60L,
  minprob = 0.05,
  maxdepth = 2L,
  mtry = c(1L, 2L),
  ntree = c(6L, 10L)
)

forest_grid
```


`tune_ci_forest()` scores each forest through a surrogate CI tree fitted to the
forest predictions on the validation fold. That is why forest tuning can still
report concentration-index validation gain.
```{r tune-forest}
forest_tuning <- tune_ci_forest(
  formula = ci_formula,
  data = tuning_data,
  rank_name = "wealth",
  outcome_name = "deadu5_num",
  weights = tuning_data$sample_weight,
  type = criterion_types,
  control_grid = forest_grid,
  v = 3L,
  strata = "deadu5_num",
  seed = 20260522,
  metrics = tuning_metrics,
  prediction_name = "forest_risk",
  parallel_over = "none",
  control = control_ci_tune(save_pred = TRUE),
  refit = TRUE
)
```


```{r forest-wide-metrics}
forest_metrics_wide <- ci_collect_metrics(
  forest_tuning,
  metric = tuning_metrics,
  format = "wide"
)

knitr::kable(
  forest_metrics_wide[
    order(-mean_validation_gain),
    .(
      type,
      ntree,
      mtry,
      minbucket,
      mean_validation_gain,
      std_err_validation_gain,
      mean_validation_relative_gain,
      mean_brier
    )
  ],
  digits = 4,
  caption = "Cross-validated forest metrics"
)
```


```{r forest-select-best}
forest_best <- ci_select_best(
  forest_tuning,
  metric = "validation_gain"
)

knitr::kable(
  forest_best[
    ,
    .(type, ntree, mtry, minsplit, minbucket, maxdepth)
  ],
  digits = 4,
  caption = "Selected forest controls"
)
```


```{r forest-fit-summary}
forest_fit_summary <- ci_fit_summary_table(
  forest_tuning,
  selected = forest_best,
  metrics = c(
    "train_gain",
    "validation_gain",
    "train_relative_gain",
    "relative_validation_gain"
  ),
  include_percent = TRUE
)

knitr::kable(
  forest_fit_summary[
    ,
    .(
      type,
      mean_root_objective,
      mean_train_gain,
      mean_percent_train_gain,
      mean_validation_gain,
      mean_percent_validation_gain,
      std_err_validation_gain
    )
  ],
  digits = 4,
  caption = "Selected forest fit summary"
)
```


With `refit = TRUE`, the final fitted forest and its surrogate tree are stored
on the tuning object.
```{r selected-forest-objects}
ci_forest_summary(forest_tuning$best_fit)

ci_tree_terminal_summary(forest_tuning$best_surrogate)[
  ,
  .(node, n, depth, outcome_percent, rule)
]
```


## Minimal Template

For most analyses, the tuning pattern is:
```{r minimal-template}
#| eval: no

grid <- ci_tree_control_grid(
  minsplit = c(100L, 200L),
  minbucket = c(50L, 100L),
  maxdepth = 2:4
)

tuned <- tune_ci_tree(
  formula = cbind(rank, outcome) ~ x1 + x2 + x3,
  data = analysis_data,
  rank_name = "rank",
  outcome_name = "outcome",
  weights = analysis_data$weights,
  type = c("CI", "CIg"),
  control_grid = grid,
  v = 5L,
  strata = "outcome",
  metrics = c("validation_gain", "relative_validation_gain"),
  control = control_ci_tune(save_pred = TRUE),
  refit = TRUE
)

selected <- ci_select_best(tuned, metric = "validation_gain")

ci_fit_summary_table(
  tuned,
  selected = selected,
  metrics = c(
    "train_gain",
    "validation_gain",
    "train_relative_gain",
    "relative_validation_gain"
  )
)
```


Use `tune_ci_forest()` with a grid that includes `ntree` and `mtry` when you
want the same cross-validation workflow for forests.
